name: compose
context: Define and run multi-container applications with Docker Compose
info: Docker Compose defines multi-container applications in a `docker-compose.yml` (or `compose.yml`) file. Services declare their image or build context, environment variables, port mappings, volume mounts, network connections, and dependency ordering. Compose V2 is integrated into the Docker CLI as `docker compose` (no hyphen). Profiles allow selective service startup for different environments (development, testing, production). Health checks ensure services are ready before dependents start. Compose handles service discovery via DNS — containers reach each other by service name. This skill covers writing Compose files, managing service lifecycles, and orchestrating development and CI environments.
body:
# Docker Compose

Docker Compose defines and runs multi-container applications from a single YAML file. Each service declares its image (or build context), configuration, dependencies, and resource constraints. Compose handles networking, volume management, and service lifecycle.

## Compose File

```yaml
# compose.yml (or docker-compose.yml)
services:
  web:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile
      target: production
    ports:
      - "8080:3000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgres://db:5432/myapp
      - REDIS_URL=redis://cache:6379
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
      cache:
        condition: service_started
    networks:
      - app-network
    restart: unless-stopped

  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp
      POSTGRES_USER: app
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U app"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5
    networks:
      - app-network

  cache:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    networks:
      - app-network

volumes:
  pgdata:

networks:
  app-network:
    driver: bridge
```

## Services

Services are the core building block. Each service defines:

- **image** or **build** — pre-built image or build from Dockerfile (see [[docker/build]])
- **ports** — host:container port mapping (see [[docker/networking#port-mapping]])
- **environment** — environment variables or `env_file` reference
- **volumes** — named volumes or bind mounts (see [[docker/volumes]])
- **depends_on** — service startup ordering with optional health conditions
- **networks** — which networks the service joins (see [[docker/networking#network-types]])
- **restart** — restart policy: `no`, `always`, `on-failure`, `unless-stopped`

## Volumes and Networks

Compose creates named volumes and networks declared in the top-level `volumes:` and `networks:` sections. Services reference them by name. Compose automatically creates a default network for the project — all services can reach each other by service name via DNS.

For persistent data, declare named volumes. See [[docker/volumes#named-volumes]] for details. For network isolation, create separate networks. See [[docker/networking#network-isolation]] for patterns.

## Profiles

Profiles activate subsets of services for different workflows:

```yaml
services:
  web:
    # always starts (no profile)
    build: .
    ports: ["8080:3000"]

  debug:
    profiles: ["debug"]
    image: busybox
    command: top

  test-runner:
    profiles: ["test"]
    build:
      context: .
      target: test
    command: npm test
```

```bash
docker compose up                    # starts only 'web'
docker compose --profile debug up    # starts 'web' + 'debug'
docker compose --profile test up     # starts 'web' + 'test-runner'
```

## Health Checks

Health checks verify that a service is ready to accept traffic:

```yaml
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:3000/health"]
  interval: 10s
  timeout: 5s
  retries: 3
  start_period: 30s
```

Use `depends_on` with `condition: service_healthy` to wait for dependencies to pass their health check before starting dependent services.

## Common Commands

```bash
docker compose up -d              # start all services in background
docker compose down               # stop and remove containers, networks
docker compose logs -f web        # follow logs for a service
docker compose ps                 # list running services
docker compose exec web sh        # shell into a running service
docker compose build              # rebuild images
docker compose pull               # pull latest images
```

sections.title: Compose file format
sections.body: The Compose file (`compose.yml` or `docker-compose.yml`) declares services, volumes, networks, and configs. Compose V2 uses the `services:` top-level key. The file supports variable interpolation via `${VAR}` syntax and `.env` files. Use `docker compose config` to validate and view the resolved configuration.

sections.title: Defining services
sections.body: Each service declares its image or build context (see [[docker/build]]), ports, environment, volumes (see [[docker/volumes]]), networks (see [[docker/networking]]), depends_on ordering, restart policy, and resource limits. Services communicate by name via DNS on shared networks. Use `scale` or `deploy.replicas` for multiple instances.

sections.title: Volumes and networks in Compose
sections.body: Declare named volumes in the top-level `volumes:` section and reference them in services. Compose creates a default bridge network for the project. Declare additional networks in the `networks:` section for isolation. See [[docker/volumes]] for volume types and [[docker/networking]] for network drivers.

sections.title: Compose profiles
sections.body: Assign profiles to services that should only start in specific workflows (debug, test, monitoring). Services without a profile always start. Activate profiles with `--profile name` on the command line. Multiple profiles can be activated simultaneously. Useful for separating development tools from production services.

sections.title: Health checks
sections.body: Define health checks with `test`, `interval`, `timeout`, `retries`, and `start_period`. The test command runs inside the container — use `CMD-SHELL` for shell commands or `CMD` for exec form. Combine with `depends_on: condition: service_healthy` to ensure dependencies are ready before starting dependent services. See also the `HEALTHCHECK` Dockerfile instruction in [[docker/build#best-practices]].
